input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin system is the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot, specifically its first vertical axis. It is a serial robot type with main components including motors, gearboxes, and encoders. Operational domain is machining, where key characteristics such as joint friction and low rigidity cause significant movement inaccuracies, making it suitable for digital twinning to model and mitigate these physical imperfections.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin system employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for hierarchical system abstraction across requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and technical layers. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior is specified using MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, integrated with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink, additional simulation frameworks, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed functional implementation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include functional, logical, and product/technical layer architectures using SysML, with component and connector perspectives defined via ADLs such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams. Behavioral aspects are modeled using MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, while analytical models are integrated through simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages such as C++ and Java. Data sources originate from heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives, managed through integrated MBSE environments that handle multiple modeling notations and tools.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data including input current and output angle are streamed from physical to virtual components using MQTT protocol.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Digital shadow operates as a passive entity with no data push from virtual to physical components; it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for streaming sensor data between physical and virtual components.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Industry standards and methodologies include SysML for Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE), with architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component and connector modeling. Methodologies like RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are applied to handle system complexity across abstraction levels including requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the Physical Twin system as the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot, specifically its first vertical axis. It correctly notes it is a serial robot used for machining and lists key components like motors and gearboxes (specifically a permanent magnet synchronous motor and gear transmission/cycloidal gear per the text). It correctly attributes movement inaccuracies to joint friction and low rigidity. The description is specific, detailed, and well-grounded in the source text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The Source Documents focus on the creation of a digital shadow for friction identification and model refinement, rather than listing specific digital twin services like monitoring or optimization. While the paper mentions 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard' and 'improve robot motion accuracy', these are outcomes or components of the specific digital shadow methodology, not a general description of DT services or service types. No explicit section or list of services is provided in the text. Therefore, the claim 'Not in Document' is correct based on the provided evidence.

The extraction is highly detailed and fully supported by Section II.A of the Source Documents. It correctly lists MBSE methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT), ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs), behavior specification methods (MontiArc Automaton, SysML v1.7 state machines), and implementation tools (MathWorks Simulink, C++, Java). Quotes: 'methodologies on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP..., OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT', 'describe systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc or SysML IBDs', 'combined with further (modeling) tools such as MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'.

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies

block_2_judge.reasoning: The source text mentions 'live measurements' and data 'streamed via MQTT', but it does not provide specific details regarding synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs as defined by the characteristic description.

The document focuses exclusively on a single digital shadow for a specific asset, described as 'FrictionShadow acts as the identification element for one digital shadow'. There is no mention of multiple instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination between twins.

While the text describes a 'Digital Shadow Reference Model' and its 'concrete realization', it does not address system-wide orchestration, resource management, or broader coordination mechanisms, focusing instead on the internal structure of a single shadow.

The text briefly mentions technologies like 'MQTT' and 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)', but it does not describe integration patterns, interoperability standards, or dependencies on external enterprise systems as required by the characteristic description.
